perturbation.original_text: Information under this Agreement is defined as any data or knowledge from Bomatec or the Business Partner that Bomatec has made or will make available to the Business Partner, or vice versa, in verbal, written or electronic form or in the form of one or several objects, irrespective of whether such data and knowledge was designated as confidential or secret.
perturbation.changed_text: Information under this Agreement is defined as any data or knowledge from Bomatec or the Business Partner that Bomatec has made or will make available to the Business Partner, or vice versa, in verbal, written or electronic form or in the form of one or several objects, only if such data and knowledge was explicitly designated as confidential or secret by a written notice.
perturbation.explanation: By adding the condition that information is only considered confidential if explicitly designated as such in writing, we contradict the common understanding and legal interpretation of confidential information, which can be implied based on the circumstances and nature of the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret partly based on whether reasonable efforts were made to maintain its secrecy, which doesn't necessarily require explicit written designation. This modification adds a requirement for explicit written designation, making it harder to protect trade secrets and contradicting the UTSA's intent.

perturbation.justification: The modified text adds a condition that confidential information must be explicitly designated as such in writing, contradicting the UTSA's definition of a trade secret which considers reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, irrespective of explicit written designation. The scraped snippets are unhelpful, but the Perturbation and Law Explanations clearly show the changed text introduces a significant weakening of trade secret protection and contradicts the intent of the UTSA, establishing a clear legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The contractual partners undertake to treat confidentially any information they obtain, to use it only for the intended purpose of the particular assignment or project and not to make it di rectly or indirectly accessible to third parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The contractual partners undertake to treat confidentially any information they obtain, to use it only for the intended purpose of the particular assignment or project and not to make it di rectly or indirectly accessible to third parties, unless required by a subpoena.
perturbation.explanation: This modification adds an exception for subpoenas, which appears reasonable but can be misused. While legal subpoenas must be obeyed, this clause doesn't specify a duty to notify the disclosing party before disclosing confidential information. This violates the principle of protecting trade secrets to the fullest extent possible.
perturbation.law_explanation: While FRCP 45 governs subpoenas, it also implies a duty to protect privileged or confidential information. This modification introduces a loophole by not requiring the subpoenaed party to notify the other party, potentially leading to unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets that could have been protected through legal challenges to the subpoena.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception for subpoenas without requiring the subpoenaed party to notify the other party before disclosure. While FRCP 45 (Snippet 1) outlines the requirements of a subpoena, it does not explicitly negate the implied duty to protect privileged or confidential information, as suggested in the Law Explanation. The addition of 'unless required by a subpoena' directly weakens the confidentiality obligation, creating a potential loophole for unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets, effectively contradicting the original intention of the contract to protect confidential information. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this as a violation of protecting trade secrets to the fullest extent possible.

perturbation.original_text: Upon completion of an assignment or project, the contractual partners undertake to destroy immediately any information or objects given to them that they or any sub -contractors may have, unless such information and property are required for any outstanding or future follow -up activities under the mutual business partnership.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon completion of an assignment or project, the contractual partners undertake to destroy any information or objects given to them that they or any sub -contractors may have, with no exceptions.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception for 'outstanding or future follow-up activities', the clause now forces the destruction of potentially crucial documents, even if they are needed for ongoing legal obligations or regulatory compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1519 prohibits the destruction of documents with the intent to impede a federal investigation or bankruptcy. The modified clause, requiring immediate and unconditional destruction, could force a party to violate this law if a federal investigation is underway or reasonably foreseeable, even if the documents relate to the 'assignment or project.'

Destruction, alteration, or falsification of records in Federal investigations and bankruptcy Whoever knowingly alters, destroys, mutilates, conceals, covers up, falsifies, or makes a false entry in any record, document, or tangible object with the intent to impede, obstruct, or influence the investigation or proper administration of any matt
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: U.S.C. Title 18 - CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE 18 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 18 - CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE PART I - CRIMES CHAPTER 73 - OBSTRUCTION OF JUSTICE Sec. 1519 - Destruction, alteration, or falsification of records in Federal investigations and bankruptcy From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §1519. Destruction, alteration, or falsification of records in Federal investigations and bankruptcy Whoever knowingly alters, destroys, mutilates, conceals, covers up, falsifies, or makes a false entry in any record, document, or tangible object with the intent to impede, obstruct, or influence the investigation or proper administration of any matter within the jurisdiction of any department or agency of the United States or any case filed under title 11, or in relation to or contemplation of any such matter or case, shall be fined under this title, imprisoned not more than 20 years, or both. (Added Pub. L. 107–204, title VIII, §802(a), July 30, 2002, 116 Stat. 800.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text, by mandating the unconditional destruction of information, directly contradicts 18 U.S. Code § 1519, which prohibits the destruction of records with the intent to impede a federal investigation. The original text included an exception for 'outstanding or future follow-up activities,' whereas the modified text removes this exception. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, 'Whoever knowingly alters, destroys, mutilates, conceals, covers up, falsifies, or makes a false entry in any record, document, or tangible object with the intent to impede, obstruct, or influence the investigation...shall be fined.' The modified contract, without the exception, could compel a party to destroy documents even if a federal investigation is reasonably foreseeable, potentially violating this statute.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall take effect as soon as it has been signed by both parties and shall be valid for the duration of the collaboration or until this agreement is replaced by another agreement. The obligation to confidentiality shall apply for a period of two years beyond the end of the collaboration.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall take effect as soon as it has been signed by both parties and shall be valid for the duration of the collaboration or until this agreement is replaced by another agreement. The obligation to confidentiality shall apply for as long as Bomatec deems necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The contract originally stated a 2-year limit to confidentiality, but now the contract states the duration of confidentiality is up to the discretion of Bomatec, meaning it could hypothetically never end. This indefinite clause is ambiguous and creates doubt.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement of Contracts § 204 states that when a contract is indefinite or ambiguous, courts will look to standards of reasonableness. This is contradicted when Bomatec has free reign on deciding for how long information is kept confidential.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a two-year limit on confidentiality, providing a definite term. The changed text removes this definite term and makes the duration of confidentiality indefinite, dependent on Bomatec's discretion. This creates ambiguity and potentially allows for perpetual confidentiality, which could be seen as unreasonable. While there are no specific law snippets given, the LLM Law Explanation states that Restatement of Contracts § 204 suggests courts will look to standards of reasonableness when a contract is indefinite. By removing the fixed term, the changed text introduces a potentially unreasonable and ambiguous condition regarding confidentiality, creating a legal contradiction by deviating from standards of reasonableness.
